allergens: tree nuts (possible in butter chicken sauce)

serving.natural_language_portion: One large Bombay Sandwich weighing approximately 350 g, consisting of two slices of grilled or toasted white bread filled with a generous butter chicken filling, vegetables, and chutney.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Chicken sandwich with sauce on white bread (SR Legacy composite); ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods — Butter Chicken (Murgh Makhani) reference
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service; Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values from the input record (provenance: GrabFood, confidence 0.7) were cross-referenced against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy composite data for chicken sandwiches with sauce on white bread and ICMR-NIN data for butter chicken (Murgh Makhani). The input values are consistent with these approved-source composites and have been retained with medium confidence. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, potassium, and several micronutrients not reported in the input are set to -1 (data not available from approved sources for this specific preparation).

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; University of Sydney GI Database — white bread reference
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Bombay Sandwich. GI estimated at 70 based on the dominant carbohydrate vehicle being white bread (GI 70–75 per University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care 31(12):2281–2283). The butter chicken filling contributes protein and fat that moderately lower the effective GI of the composite dish; however, the white bread base dominates. GL per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (70 × 45.01) / 100 = 31.5. Carbohydrate per 350 g serving = 12.86 × 3.5 = 45.01 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 314 mg/100 g; estimated 1,100 mg per 350 g serving — approaching 50% of the 2,300 mg daily limit in a single meal
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced-salt butter chicken sauce preparation, omit or reduce cheese, and limit portion to half a sandwich to keep sodium intake below 600 mg per meal.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High-GI white bread base (GI ~70) combined with 12.86 g/100 g carbohydrate and only 1.14 g/100 g dietary fibre produces a high glycemic load of 31.5 per standard serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with type 2 diabetes should substitute white bread with wholemeal or multigrain bread to lower GI, reduce or omit potato filling if present, and limit portion size to reduce the glycemic load below 20 per meal.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 5.14 g/100 g (approximately 18 g per 350 g serving) from butter, ghee, cream, and cheese, combined with cholesterol 34.29 mg/100 g and trans fat 0.14 g/100 g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should request preparation with minimal butter or ghee, reduced cream in the sauce, and no added cheese to bring saturated fat below 10 g per serving and eliminate trans fat exposure.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Saturated fat 5.14 g/100 g at the pro-inflammatory threshold, combined with nightshade ingredients (tomato puree, capsicum) and refined white bread
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should monitor nightshade sensitivity, request reduced butter/ghee and cream to lower saturated fat, and substitute white bread with wholemeal or sourdough to reduce the refined carbohydrate pro-inflammatory load.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Bombay Sandwich is a popular Indian street-food-inspired sandwich originating from Mumbai (formerly Bombay), India, and widely served in Singapore's Indian food establishments and hawker stalls. In this Singapore variant, the sandwich features a butter chicken (murgh makhani) filling — a rich, creamy, tomato-based spiced chicken curry — layered between two slices of white bread that are grilled or toasted until golden. The filling typically includes tender chicken pieces cooked in a sauce of tomato puree, cream, butter or ghee, garam masala, cumin, coriander, and turmeric, often accompanied by sliced onion, capsicum, and a spread of coriander-mint chutney. The result is a warm, hearty sandwich with a mildly spiced, creamy, and slightly tangy flavour profile, with a crisp exterior from grilling and a soft, saucy interior. Nutritionally, per 100 g the sandwich provides approximately 186 kcal, 10 g protein, 10.9 g fat (5.1 g saturated), and 12.9 g carbohydrate with only 1.1 g dietary fibre. Sodium is 314 mg/100 g, making a full 350 g serving a moderate-to-high sodium meal. The high-GI white bread base (GI ~70) and low fibre content create a high glycemic load (GL ~31.5 per serving), making portion control important for individuals managing blood glucose. The saturated fat content from dairy ingredients is clinically relevant for cardiovascular and inflammatory conditions. This dish is suitable for halal diets and contains gluten and dairy allergens.
common_user_queries: Is Bombay Sandwich the same as Mumbai Sandwich?
What is in a Butter Chicken Sandwich?

confidence_reason: Input nutrient values sourced from GrabFood (delivery platform, confidence 0.7) are consistent with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy composite data for chicken sandwiches with sauce and ICMR-NIN butter chicken reference values. No exact approved-source match exists for Bombay Sandwich as a named dish; values are adapted from closest approved-source composites. GI is an analogous estimate based on white bread GI from the University of Sydney GI Database. Medium confidence is appropriate.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, potassium, and extended micronutrient data for this specific Bombay Sandwich preparation are not available in USDA FoodData Central or ICMR-NIN databases as of 2024. All core macros (protein_g 10.0, fat_g 10.86, carbohydrate_g 12.86) are non-zero and reflect the mixed bread-and-filling composition of this dish. Trans fat 0.14 g/100 g is a low but non-zero value consistent with trace dairy-derived trans fats from butter and ghee. The input description references both 'Bombay Sandwich' and 'Butter Chicken' — this record treats the dish as a butter chicken-filled grilled sandwich, which is the most culinarily coherent interpretation. GI estimated at 70 from white bread analogue (University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care); GL per 350 g serving = 31.5. Halal suitability assumes chicken is halal-certified; consumers should verify with the specific vendor. Ontology mapped to FoodOn FOODON_00002473 and FoodEx2 A0FKP using closest_parent match type as no exact codes exist for this dish.
